name: Integration patterns (Luxair)
description: SOA vs microservices, ESB trade-offs, sync vs async fan-out and legacy integration migration — from the Luxair interview prep docs.

topics.name: SOA & ESB
topics.description: Service-oriented architecture and enterprise service buses; central integration layers and their risks.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

SOA (service-oriented architecture) and the ESB (enterprise service bus) are the pre-microservices way of connecting systems: services communicate through a central bus that routes, transforms and orchestrates messages. Microservices are the reaction to it.

## What problem does it solve?

Enterprises had dozens of systems that all needed to talk. SOA standardized this with shared contracts and a central integration layer — one place where formats were converted and flows coordinated.

## How it works

The core difference from microservices is WHERE the integration logic lives. SOA centralizes it in the bus; microservices decentralize — "smart endpoints, dumb pipes": the logic lives in the services, the transport just carries messages.

Why the ESB became infamous as a bottleneck: business logic quietly accumulates in the bus; soon EVERY change to any flow goes through the bus — and typically through the one team that owns it. Deployments couple, the bus becomes the whole organization's critical path. This is also Conway's Law in action: a central integration team produces a central integration layer, with its coordination costs baked into the architecture.

The balanced view: SOA's ideas survived — contracts, reuse, governance — and a central integration layer still fits some enterprises (many legacy systems, heavy compliance). The modern default is decentralized ownership with events for decoupling.

## Composition vocabulary

- Orchestration vs choreography: orchestration = a central coordinator calls each step; choreography = services react to each other's events, no coordinator. Choreography decouples, but the overall flow becomes harder to SEE — observability is the price.
- Aggregator: one service fans out to several sources and composes one response — common at the API edge, so a client does not make five round trips.
- Coarse-grained services: fewer calls carrying complete business payloads — usually better across a network than chatty fine-grained calls.

## Common mistakes

- Business logic accumulating in the bus until every change funnels through one team
- Choreography everywhere with no observability — nobody can see the flow
- Dismissing central integration entirely — compliance-heavy estates still need some

## Key terms

- ESB — central bus for routing/transformation; the classic bottleneck
- Smart endpoints, dumb pipes — the microservices counter-principle
- Orchestration vs choreography — central brain vs event reactions
- Aggregator — one call in, several calls out, one composed answer

topics.name: Integration migration
topics.description: Safely migrating flows off a legacy integration platform (e.g. Mule/ESB) with behavior compatibility.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The playbook for moving integration flows off a legacy platform (an old ESB, Mule, BizTalk) onto plain services — without the systems that depend on those flows noticing anything.

## What problem does it solve?

Old integration platforms become expensive, unsupported, or a bottleneck — but dozens of business-critical flows run through them, and their consumers cannot all be updated at once.

## How it works

Strangler-style, one flow at a time:
- Keep the contract: route ONE flow to a new implementation BEHIND THE SAME interface. Consumers keep calling exactly what they called before.
- Verify behavior empirically, not by reading code: run old and new in parallel (shadow traffic), diff their outputs, replay recorded real traffic through both. Only cut over when outputs match.
- Order by risk: migrate low-risk flows first to harden the process; critical flows last.
- Keep a fast way back: switching back to the legacy flow must be a config change, not a project. Watch per-flow error rates after each cutover.

The dirty secret of such migrations: legacy flows contain UNDOCUMENTED transformations — a date format fix here, a hardcoded country mapping there — that nobody remembers. Discovery is empirical: capture real inputs and outputs, and treat every old-vs-new difference as either a bug to fix or a behavior to consciously preserve.

## Common mistakes

- Big-bang cutover of all flows at once
- Trusting the legacy code/docs instead of diffing real traffic outputs
- No instant way back — rollback as a project instead of a switch

## Key terms

- Strangler pattern — the new gradually replaces the old, piece by piece
- Same contract — consumers must not notice the swap
- Shadow / parallel run — old and new side by side, outputs compared
- Traffic replay — real recorded requests as the test suite
- Rollback switch — instant return to the legacy flow

topics.name: Async fan-out
topics.description: Notifying many systems after a business event: critical path vs asynchronous distribution.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The pattern for telling many systems about one business event: after a booking is created, five other systems need to know — and the design question is how.

## What problem does it solve?

The naive approach — the booking service calls five APIs, one after another — makes the user wait for all five, and any one failing breaks the booking. The five systems' problems become the user's problem.

## How it works

Start with the question: what does the USER have to wait for? Only that stays synchronous — persist the booking, answer "done". Everything else is not on the critical path.

Then publish ONE event — "booking-created" — to a message broker, and let the five systems SUBSCRIBE. The producer does not call anyone; it does not even know how many consumers exist. Adding a sixth system later is a new subscription, not a code change to the booking service.

What this buys, concretely:
- Failure isolation: each consumer has its own retries and its own dead-letter queue. The invoicing system being down does not affect the loyalty-points system — or the user.
- Spike absorption: the queue buffers bursts; consumers process at their own pace.
- Duplicates handled: brokers deliver at-least-once, so consumers check "have I processed this eventId already?" (idempotency).

The exception that proves the rule: if a step must gate the outcome — a fraud check that must PASS before the booking is confirmed — it belongs on the synchronous path by definition. Sync vs async is decided by business dependency, not preference.

Why Node.js keeps showing up in integration services: this work is I/O-bound — many concurrent network calls, little computation — which a non-blocking event loop handles cheaply. Its weakness is CPU-heavy work, which blocks the loop and stalls every request; that belongs in worker threads or a separate service.

## Common mistakes

- The producer calling five consumer APIs in sequence — the user waits for all
- One consumer's failure blocking the others (shared retry/DLQ)
- Fraud-check-style gating steps pushed async when they must gate the outcome
- No idempotency although the broker delivers at-least-once

## Key terms

- Critical path — only what the user must wait for
- Publish/subscribe — one event out, any number of consumers
- Failure isolation — per-consumer retries and DLQs
- At-least-once + idempotency — duplicates arrive; handle them once
- I/O-bound vs CPU-bound — where Node.js shines vs struggles

questions.title: SOA vs microservices, and the ESB bottleneck
questions.prompt: What is the difference between SOA and microservices — and why can an ESB become a bottleneck?
questions.answerStructureHint: Shared bus and smart pipes vs smart endpoints and dumb pipes; central logic accumulation, coupling and single point of failure.

expectedPoints.label: Core architectural difference
expectedPoints.description: SOA centralizes integration logic (ESB, shared contracts); microservices favor decentralized ownership — smart endpoints, dumb pipes.

expectedPoints.label: Why the ESB becomes a bottleneck
expectedPoints.description: Business logic accumulates in the bus; every change funnels through one team/platform; scaling and deploys couple everything.

expectedPoints.label: Balanced, not dogmatic
expectedPoints.description: Notes SOA ideas live on (contracts, reuse) and central integration still fits some enterprises.

followUps.prompt: How does Conway's Law show up in this choice?

questions.title: Notify five systems after a booking
questions.prompt: After a booking is created, five other systems must be notified. Sync or async — how would you design it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Keep the critical path minimal, publish one event and fan out async; handle per-consumer failure, ordering and duplicates.

expectedPoints.label: Minimal critical path
expectedPoints.description: Only what the user must wait for stays synchronous (persist booking); notifications are not on the critical path.
acceptedSignals: five sequential calls would be slow

expectedPoints.label: Publish once, fan out via broker
expectedPoints.description: Emit one booking-created event; consumers subscribe independently instead of the producer calling five APIs.

expectedPoints.label: Per-consumer failure isolation
expectedPoints.description: Each consumer has its own retries/DLQ; one failing system doesn't block the others; duplicates handled idempotently.

followUps.prompt: Which of the five would you still call synchronously if, say, fraud check must pass before confirming?

questions.title: Migrating off a legacy integration platform
questions.prompt: You must migrate integration flows from a legacy platform (like Mule/ESB) to plain services. How do you do it without breaking consumers?
questions.answerStructureHint: Inventory and characterize flows, migrate incrementally behind stable contracts, verify behavior parity (shadow traffic), keep rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Incremental, flow by flow
expectedPoints.description: Strangler-style: route one flow at a time to the new implementation behind the same contract, not a big-bang rewrite.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies behavior compatibility
expectedPoints.description: Compares old vs new outputs — shadow/parallel runs, contract tests, replaying real traffic — before cutover.

expectedPoints.label: Risk ordering and rollback
expectedPoints.description: Start with low-risk flows, keep a fast way back, monitor error rates per migrated flow.

followUps.prompt: Legacy ESB flows often contain undocumented transformations. How do you discover what the flow really does?

questions.title: Node.js for integration services
questions.prompt: Why is Node.js a good fit for integration/API services, and where is it a poor choice?
questions.answerStructureHint: Event loop excels at I/O-bound concurrent calls; single thread suffers on CPU-bound work; mitigation via workers.

expectedPoints.label: Strong for I/O-bound work
expectedPoints.description: Non-blocking event loop handles many concurrent network calls cheaply — exactly what integration services do.

expectedPoints.label: Weak for CPU-bound work
expectedPoints.description: A single-threaded loop blocks on heavy computation, stalling every request.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the mitigations
expectedPoints.description: Worker threads, offloading to services/queues, or picking another runtime for compute-heavy paths.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect event-loop blocking in a production Node.js service?

questions.title: 100M requests a month — now what?
questions.prompt: A service is expected to take 100 million requests a month. Walk me through the back-of-envelope sizing and the questions you would ask.

expectedPoints.label: Does the arithmetic
expectedPoints.description: ≈39 rps average; states the formula and computes.

expectedPoints.label: Average vs peak + request cost
expectedPoints.description: Peak can be many times average; cache hit vs heavy query; downstream/DB limits bound the system.

expectedPoints.label: Concurrency formula
expectedPoints.description: In-flight ≈ rps × latency seconds; slow downstream multiplies concurrency and eats pools.

expectedPoints.label: Sizes on percentiles
expectedPoints.description: p95/p99 over averages for sizing and alerting.
